sentences.text: Well, we was talking about my sister's school.

Normally, after having spent the first four grades at a folkshul, which is like an elementary school, there was a separation of the people who stayed in the elementary school to the eighth grade and then went to become an apprentice in some trade, or those who went on to higher learning.
The plan had been for my sister to go to a gymnasium, which normally, in Germany, start at the fifth grade.
They did in those days, anyway.
Well, the-- the gymnasium in Cologne was no longer for Jews.
Jews were not allowed to go to the regular gymnasium.
However, there was a Jewish school in Cologne called the Yavneh.
And my sister went to the Yavneh.
Now, the Yavneh was a relatively religious school.
It was quite Orthodox.
So that was a bit difficult for my sister.

But there were so many non-Orthodox Jewish children going to the Yavneh, because they couldn't go anywhere else anymore; so that she soon fit in very nicely, and had a circle of friends, and did very well.
So this was a school which really prepared Jewish children for higher learning.
And-- but it was a Jewish school, and it was only for the Jewish people.
So this was a real change.
Normally, my parents wouldn't have sent her to a school like the Yavneh.
But there was no other way.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How did things change?
How did you know that your sister could no longer go to the school she originally would have gone to?
I don't know exactly how it happened.
But obviously, my-- when my parents-- I think, when they applied to the other school, they were told that they don't take Jewish children.
And that was the end of that.
And I think in those days, all the Jewish children who were in those schools were told that they could no longer continue.
It wasn't such a big trip.
There was a-- a train, a commuter train that ran between Cologne and Bonn, the other large city.
And we lived in between.
Bonn is also on the Rhine, and naturally, Cologne is on the Rhine.
And we lived in a little village which is very close to Cologne.
So it was maybe a 15, 20 minute trip on this commuter train--
--to the school.
In Koln-- in--
--in Cologne.
The school was in Cologne.
My sister went every day to Cologne to go to school.
And that was not unusual, because it wasn't-- it wasn't much of a trip.
As a matter of fact, today, Surth is really part of Cologne.
I mean, the city has spread to encompass this village.
I've been back there, and there is no definition anymore.
[LAUGHS]
It-- it's just a sprawl.
If there were only three Jewish families in Surth--
Yes.
--and this school in Cologne was the only school available for Jewish--
Children.
Oh, yes.
No, no, no.
This was a large school.
And there was a large Jewish population in Cologne.
I don't know exactly how many people.
As a child, I wasn't aware of that.
But also, very quickly, my father could no longer belong to the gymnastics club in Surth, which he used to belong to.
And they didn't-- Jews couldn't stay there.
And he joined the-- the Jewish gymnastics club-- the Judische [NON-ENGLISH], it was called, [LAUGHS] in Cologne.
And he went regularly to do gymnastics there.
Well, the business was doing all right.
It was-- as a matter of fact, this was our salvation.
And that's why we came to the United States.
Somehow, his customers didn't want to abandon him.
Even his non-- well, there weren't any Jewish customers.
We did not have.
We didn't have.
There were only three Jewish families.
You can't run a business on that, locally.
[LAUGHS]
So we-- we didn't have a kosher butcher store.
This was a regular German butcher store where you carried pork and-- you know, in the store.
It was just a general butcher store.
And somehow, the customers were pretty loyal, and they stayed.
This was fine for the first year, or two, or three.
And then things started to get tough, because there were other butcher stores in Surth.
And they really wanted to get my father out of business.
So in 1935, I believe it was-- I am not 100% sure of the year-- our store was closed.
And my father was arrested.
It was at a time where Jews were not generally arrested.
But he was arrested-- and on some pretext that we didn't observe all the rules of cleanliness, or some such thing.
And my father was held in the local jail in Cologne for three days.
Well, this was the shock that he needed.
He came out of there.
And he said to my mother, we are getting out of here.
But it wasn't all that easy.
At the time, my mother's sister had-- my Aunt Erna-- had gone to the United States, had moved to the United States.
My mother had an aunt in the United States.
And they had made out papers for Aunt Erna.
Aunt Erna was single.
She was a woman in her thirties.
And she was pretty adventurous.
And she had wanted to get out of there and go to America to live.
So she had-- she was just about, at that time, leaving Germany when my father was arrested and the store was closed.
The store did not get reopened for some time after he was released from jail.
He-- I know he was out of-- he was gone for only three days.
But I think it was months before we could open the store again.
They just-- whatever.
They-- they kept on saying that this thing hasn't been resolved properly, and whatever.
I don't know exactly what the particulars were.
But they were just trumped up charges to keep the store closed.
The-- the officials.
In those days, the Nazis were in power in Germany.
And they were doing everything to get Jews out of business.
And--
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
What other changes did I see?

Besides the-- the SS marching, and the SR marching, and the kids marching and singing, and the-- the newspapers carrying stories-- especially this new newspaper, the which, of course, we didn't buy, but was posted on the walls-- telling people that the Jews are such terrible people.
And they have to be eliminated somehow.
And you don't do business with Jews.
And there were caricatures of-- of-- of Jews, you know, with the typical big noses and-- it was all so ugly.
And it was all around us.
No.
They were posted.
[DRINKING]
I didn't sense so much, except that they were always-- there was always this talk at home-- what are we going to do?
Is this thing going to last?
Isn't it going to last?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father had a very large family.
He was the youngest-- excuse me-- of nine children.
And I think he was the only one who really felt that things were not going to get any better.
No.
Oh, no.
They-- they were in the general area, but not in Surth.
We had-- I had aunts and uncles in the neighboring town, Rodenkirchen.
And I had aunts and uncles.
And I had-- one family was in Rheinbach, one in Schweiz.
All of these towns were, I would say, within one or two hours of driving.
So we used to get together for parties, birthday parties.
We used to celebrate my parents' birthday all the time.
We didn't have any kids' parties in those days.
[LAUGHS] But for my parents' birthday and for the other people's birthdays, we would go there.
And of course, there were bar mitzvahs.
There were weddings.
And the family always got together.
And when the family got together, and we would visit, the talk was almost always about the politics, what's going on.
And there were some Jewish people who were joining the Hashara.
Now, the Hashara was the Jewish organization to go and work on a kibbutz in Israel.
And here, something did develop.
I don't know exactly when it was.
Maybe it was 1934 or '35-- maybe it were-- I don't know exactly-- when non-Jews could no longer work for Jews.
And of course, that meant that Ketchen, my very dear housekeeper, and Fritz-- Fritz worked in the store, and--
Fritz was the man who worked in the store with my father.
Non-Jewish man, yes.
And they lived in our house.
They were sleep-in help.
And they were really part of the family.
And unfortunately, they had to leave.
[LAUGHS] And actually, I went back to visit them in 1970.
Now that was--
--a very happy reunion.
It really was.
And they were still in Rheinbach.
And they had grown children of their own by then.
But I never saw the children.
I just saw the-- the two of them.
So then we got a new housekeeper.
And this was a Jewish girl from Berlin who was studying-- in training, I should say-- to go to Israel.
We got her through the Hashara movement.
And many of the young Jewish women who had never done any housework had to be trained to do housework, and to work [LAUGHS] before they went to Israel.
That was-- that seemed to be the thing.
Well, this was a lovely gal, who I know my mother complained about a lot, because she wasn't a very good housekeeper.
She couldn't do the cleaning and whatever like Ketchen used to.
But anyway, I liked her, because she-- the most wonderful thing she did was send for all her children's books to Berlin and provided me with reading material.
And I used to love to read.
And it was an absolute treasure.
And I thought this gal was terrific.
And I had enough to read until we left Germany.
I got this huge box of books, I remember.
So for me, it wasn't as-- that-- that was a pretty good development.
Somehow I always got along with people who were working in the house.
But that was also a major thing, that you couldn't-- you couldn't have non-Jews working for you anymore.
How did Ketchen and Fritz wind up leaving?
Did the military come in and say, you have to leave?
How did everybody know that they had to quit, and how did the quitting take place?
I don't know exactly.
Maybe it was we got a letter from the headquarters or wherever, and said Jews are no longer permitted and by such and such a date they have to leave.
It must have been something like that.
Nobody came to the door and said, you can no longer work.
But we knew it was now illegal to employ non-Jews.
Oh, tearfully.
[LAUGHS] I remember that, you know, I hated to see her go.
And I felt terrible.
It-- it was like a part of my life was changing.
And the nice thing was that they were getting married.
We knew that, you know, she and Fritz were getting married, and they were starting their own business.
And they left to go to Rheinbach.
And we had relatives in Rheinbach.
So I did see them again.
I saw them again.
And as a matter of fact, later on, there-- after we had left Germany already, a number of my other relatives, in addition to the ones who were living in this town, also moved to Rheinbach.
And they gave Ketchen and Fritz some of their valuables before they were deported to the concentration camp.

And on that visit to Germany, in 1970, when I visited them, they gave me some things for Alice, my cousin, Alice, who-- whose parents had been in Rheinbach and whose sister had been in Rheinbach before they were deported.
Oh, there was some silver, some crystal, just household things.
And I gave them to my cousin.
No.
Not that I remember.
Not that I remember.
No.
No, no.
I saw her again just before we left, because I visited the relatives in Rheinbach, and I saw them when I was there.
So the-- the break was not total.
And-- and-- and for me, as a child, anyway, it isn't like they-- I could no longer see them.
I saw them.
Oh, yes.
I understood that.
I understood that they could no longer work for Jews.
And now we had to find Jewish help, and this was going to be difficult.
So-- yeah.
And after-- going back to when my father was arrested and the store was closed-- the store was closed for a number of months.
And-- but eventually, we were allowed to reopen the store.
And we needed help in order to slaughter animals, and make sausages, and all the things my father had to do.
He needed help.
So my cousin, Fritz, came to work for us as an apprentice.
And my father was always very, very fond of Fritz.
And it's amusing that both the man who worked for us was named Fritz, and my cousin was also Fritz.
But-- What was Fritz-- do you remember any of these people's last names-- Ketchen's last name or the two Fritz' last names?
Well, the second one was Fritz Wolf, because he was a-- a member of the family.
But I don't remember.
Offhand, I just-- it escapes me what the last name was.
Maybe by the end of the interview [LAUGHS] I'll remember--
--but not right now, I don't.
So anyway, that closed that chapter.
So--
What other changes?
I continued to go to the Catholic school.
I continued to go to all my classes and still got excused from the religious instruction, played with the local children.
I played with the non-Jewish children right up until the end.
I remember going into the church with them on Christmas, and things that my parents might have frowned upon, but I went ahead and did anyhow.
And actually, I-- I felt very warm to some of these Catholic observances, because there-- there was no animosity there.
It felt safe.
The religious aspect felt safe.
It was the outside, the brown shirts, the marching, and-- that was ugly, that felt ugly.
So I had this dichotomy.
The school was safe.
The teachers were kind.
They certainly wouldn't permit any of the children to mistreat me in school.
And they didn't.
And I felt safe going into the church.
And yet there was this-- this movement afoot.
After all, my father was put in jail.
My-- the store was closed.
The-- the placards on the door, the swastikas, they was-- it was all around us.
It was there, you know, on the outside.
But still, with all that was going on, there were still Jews-- many of them, even in our own family-- that felt that you shouldn't leave.
I mean, how can you leave?

How can you leave the place where you make a living, and you're comfortable, and-- and everything is-- and you're established, and go off to some other country where you don't know the language, where you don't know what to do?
I remember sitting in the kitchen of my Aunt Else-- that's my father's older sister.
Vasen-- V-A-S-E-N.
They lived in Rodenkirchen, which was the closest to where we lived.
And we had gone there for the afternoon.
And my Aunt Else, arguing with my father, because my father had said, I am leaving.
I'm taking my family out of here.
It's not-- there is no future here.
And she said, how can you be so irresponsible?
You are leaving a place where you have a livelihood, where you can take care of your family.
And you're going off into nowhere.
What are you-- are you crazy?
I mean, you don't do things like that!
And I remember that there was this argument.
And I was sitting there and listening to it.
It's very clear in my mind.
But my father was determined.
He was going to get out of there.
And he was now doing everything he could to leave.
He had a cousin in the United States.
These cousins were quite well-to-do.
And my mother's sister, who had emigrated in 1935 or thereabouts, my Aunt Erna, was to look up these cousins, because they had money.
They were in business, and they could probably vouch for us so that we could get a visa to come to the United States.
And in fact, when Aunt Erna went to see them-- and this probably was in 1935 or so-- they said that they would.
But then we got a letter.
And incidentally, my parents were going to Berlitz to study English in-- with the idea of coming to the United States.
In Koln.
In Cologne.
There was a Berlitz school.
And they took English courses.
My sister was studying English.
And they were going to come to the United States.
But then, as I just said, there was a letter that came from our cousins, my father's cousins.

And they said that they're terribly sorry, but they reconsidered that it is such a responsibility to get four people from Europe to the United States, that they felt that they couldn't see themselves clear to undertake this.
And they called the whole thing off.
And so we were stuck.
But my father wasn't going to stop that-- stop him from trying to get out of the United-- out of Germany.
And he inquired what different countries were offering, where there was free immigration, where there was a possibility of going.
And he finally decided that we were going to go to Uruguay, in South America, to Montevideo.
It seems that Uruguay had some kind of a law that-- like the American homestead law-- that if you wanted to be a farmer, they would give you land to cultivate.
And you could immigrate into Uruguay without having anyone vouch for you or anything.
It was an open country.
And my father says, that's the only way to get out.
He'll get out.
Incidentally, he didn't want to go to Europe anywhere, because he felt that he had to get away from Europe.
He-- he was afraid.
And this was-- and I don't know why he felt that, but he was not about to go to France or to England or anywhere like that.
He wanted to get far away.
So we couldn't go to the United States.
But he was going to go to Uruguay.
And they switched at the Berlitz school from English lessons to Spanish lessons.
I remember that very well.
I didn't get any lessons, because they felt, well, I'm too young.
[LAUGHS] I'm not going to-- I'll learn whenever I get there.
That was the attitude.
So-- but my sister was studying, because they felt that she probably would have to go to work or something, and she needed to know the language of wherever we were going.
And we had all kinds of material in the house.
I remember reading the-- the-- the folders, and looking at the pictures of going to South America.
And we were all set.
And that was that.
In the meantime, my father had found out that you couldn't take any money out of Germany.
There were rules that you could only leave with 10 marks or something like that.
Jews were not allowed to take any money officially out of Germany.
Even if they emigrated, they-- they could not take any money out.
So somehow he managed to get a sum of money out of Germany into Switzerland.
And don't ask me how.
[LAUGHS] There must have been ways, and he found out how to do it.
So we had some money now in Switzerland.
And Aunt Erna spoke to the aunt and uncle-- that is, from her side of the family.
This is my mother's side of the family-- no longer my father's cousin.
Because Aunt Erna wanted us to come to New York, where she was.
She didn't want us to go to Uruguay.
She was doing anything.
So she wrote that if we can manage to get the money that's in Switzerland to the United States, she would put it on the aunt and uncle's name.
And they would have enough money, she found out, that it would cover us to come to the United States.

So then we abandoned Uruguay and tried to get Aunt Rose and Uncle Ike, that's-- Aunt Rose was my grandmother's sister on my mother's side of the family-- and Uncle Ike to make the papers for us, along with Aunt Erna.
She already was working as a housekeeper somewhere in New York.
And that-- that worked.
Somehow, they got the papers together without the rich cousins.
And we had to go to Stuttgart for the visa.
Now this happened over the course of 1936, '37.
It all took a lot of time.
It didn't happen suddenly.
The money had to be transferred first, and then they had enough to allow us to come here.
Because these were poor people.
They-- they didn't have much money.
My aunt worked as a maid.
My-- my mother's aunt and uncle, the old people, had a little newsstand in one of the-- one of the buildings here in Manhattan.
They lived in Astoria, in Queens.
But it worked.
We got the visa.
We were early enough to be on the quota, and we came here.
